ts.sec: The Good Future: Abundance in 5 Years

turns.en: So anyway, I guess the good future is you can have whatever stuff you want and incredible medical care that's better than any medical care that exists. So I think if you lift your gaze to not a super distant point, five years from now, four years from now, maybe we'll have better medical care than anyone has today available for everyone within 5 years.
turns.zh: 总之，我猜美好的未来是你可以拥有任何你想要的东西，以及比现有任何医疗都更好的医疗。所以我认为如果你把目光放远一点，不是特别遥远的未来，五年后、四年后，也许我们会在五年内让每个人都获得比今天任何人都更好的医疗。

turns.en: No scarcity of goods or services. The best education available for everybody. You can learn anything you want about anything for free.
turns.zh: 没有商品或服务的稀缺。每个人都能获得最好的教育。你可以免费学习任何你想学的东西。

turns.en: What about access to compute?
turns.zh: 那算力访问呢？

turns.en: People will probably care a lot more about that than their government check in about three years.
turns.zh: 大约三年后，人们可能对算力的关心程度会远超对政府支票的关心。

turns.en: Well, what do they want to do with compute?
turns.zh: 他们想用算力做什么？

turns.en: Well, compute translates to anything you want, right? Your virtual friend, your entertainment, it's probably everything.
turns.zh: 算力可以转化为你想要的任何东西，对吧？你的虚拟朋友、你的娱乐，它可能是一切。

turns.en: Those are AI services basically.
turns.zh: 那些基本上就是 AI 服务。

turns.en: Yeah. Or your ability to innovate, too. You can't innovate without an AI assistant at that point.
turns.zh: 是的。或者你的创新能力。到那时，没有 AI 助手你就无法创新。

turns.en: One of our other moonshot mates, Ismael, asked this question. He said: Elon, you often say physics is the law, everything else is a recommendation. So as AI, energy, and space systems scale exponentially, what non-physical constraints—organizational, cultural, bureaucratic, or human—are now the real bottleneck? Is there a bottleneck?
turns.zh: 我们的另一位登月伙伴 Ismael 问了这个问题。他说：Elon，你常说物理是定律，其他都是建议。那么随着 AI、能源和太空系统呈指数级扩展，哪些非物理约束——组织、文化、官僚或人力——现在成了真正的瓶颈？有瓶颈吗？

turns.en: Electricity generation is the limiting factor. The innermost loop. I think people are underestimating the difficulty of bringing electricity online. You've got to generate the electricity, you need transformers for the transformers, so you have to convert that voltage to something the computers can digest. You've got to cool the computers. So it's basically electricity generation and cooling that are limiting factors for AI.
turns.zh: 发电是限制因素。最内层的循环。我认为人们低估了电力上线的难度。你必须发电，你需要变压器的变压器，所以你必须把电压转换成计算机能消化的东西。你还得冷却计算机。所以基本上发电和冷却才是 AI 的限制因素。

turns.en: And once you have humanoid robotics, they can address the power generation and the cooling stuff. But that is the limiting factor and will be for at least the next two years.
turns.zh: 一旦有了人形机器人，它们就能解决发电和冷却问题。但那是限制因素，并且至少在未来两年内都是。

turns.en: Isn't it amazing how divergent the Memphis version of that is from the space-based version? You have solar panels in common, but otherwise no storage, abundant amounts of energy.
turns.zh: 孟菲斯版本和太空版本的分歧是不是很惊人？你们都有太阳能板，但除此之外，没有储能，能量充足。

turns.en: But you have launch costs and weight suddenly matters. I don't care too much about the weight in Tennessee. Suddenly the weight is a critical factor. Those two pathways for compute have a huge divergence from here forward.
turns.zh: 但你有发射成本，重量突然变得重要。在田纳西我不太在意重量。突然重量就成了关键因素。这两种算力路径从这里开始会有巨大分歧。

ts.sec: AI Compute in Space vs. Earth

turns.en: Once we get solar domestically at scale and if we're launching Starship at scale, then by far the cheapest way to do AI compute will be in space. Once you have full and complete reusability, the propellant cost per flight is maybe a million dollars.
turns.zh: 一旦我们在国内大规模部署太阳能，并且大规模发射星舰，那么迄今为止最便宜的 AI 计算方式将是在太空中。一旦你实现了完全可重复使用，每次飞行的推进剂成本可能只有一百万美元。

turns.en: People don't realize how much it costs. So if you listen, it's called a million dollars of transport for 10 megawatts of AI compute.
turns.zh: 人们没意识到成本有多高。所以如果你听，这叫一百万美元的运输成本对应 10 兆瓦的 AI 算力。

turns.en: Assuming everything keeps trending the way it's currently trending, if you look at the next four years of accelerating launches, 200 tons per launch. Thousands where you're going, but if say sunny, it's probably more like 150 tons. But it's the right order of magnitude—at least in excess of 100 tons for a marginal cost per flight of around a million.
turns.zh: 假设一切保持当前趋势，如果你看未来四年加速发射，每次发射 200 吨。你要去的地方有数千次，但如果说晴天，可能更像 150 吨。但数量级是对的——每次飞行边际成本约一百万美元，至少超过 100 吨。

turns.en: So what fraction of all that launched mass is data centers in space as opposed to moon base, launch to Mars? Interesting how this is a new space objective we weren't talking about even a year ago.
turns.zh: 那么所有发射质量中有多少是太空数据中心，而不是月球基地、发射到火星？有趣的是，这是一个新的太空目标，甚至一年前我们都没在讨论。

turns.en: All of a sudden, data centers have become the massive driving force for opening up space, and also the urgent use case too.
turns.zh: 突然间，数据中心成了开拓太空的巨大驱动力，也是紧迫的用例。

turns.en: I used to wonder what's going to drive humanity. I thought it was asteroid mining, right? You were focused on Mars.
turns.zh: 我以前想知道什么会驱动人类。我以为是小行星采矿，对吧？你专注于火星。

turns.en: We will actually want to mine asteroids to turn them into photovoltaic before you know, not for anything else. I mean if we're going to build out Dyson swarms, just a bunch of satellites around the sun.
turns.zh: 我们实际上会想开采小行星，把它们变成光伏板，你知道，不是为了别的。我的意思是，如果我们要建造戴森球，就是一堆围绕太阳的卫星。

turns.en: Another question Alex wanted us to ask: what's your time frame for humanity achieving a Dyson swarm? Is it 50 years?
turns.zh: Alex 还想让我们问一个问题：人类实现戴森球的时间框架是什么？是 50 年吗？

turns.en: How big is this? It's a matter. Dyson swarm—people think everything's just going to be covered in satellites. I think it's not quite that. I mean, what mass ends up becoming satellite. Mercury probably ends up being satellites. Jupiter, Saturn. It's a little gassy. It's big but there's a lot of rocks orbiting. Do you leave Mars alone? Yeah, leave Mars alone. Asteroids are fantastic food source. No gravity. Well, gravity well on Jupiter is a non-issue. Already mostly differentiated into carbonaceous chondrites for fuel and nickel-iron for materials, gold. A bunch of the asteroid belt probably turns into solar panels, star power.
turns.zh: 这有多大？是个问题。戴森球——人们以为一切都会被卫星覆盖。我认为不完全是那样。我的意思是，什么质量最终会成为卫星。水星可能最终变成卫星。木星、土星。它有点气态。它很大但有很多岩石在轨道上。你会留下火星吗？是的，留下火星。小行星是极好的食物来源。没有重力。嗯，木星的重力井不是问题。已经大部分分化成碳质球粒陨石作为燃料，镍铁作为材料，还有金。小行星带的一大块可能变成太阳能板，恒星能量。

turns.en: I've known you for 26 years now. It feels to me like you've gotten much smarter or much more capable over this last decade. Do you feel that way? Do you feel like you just have better people around you, better tools? What's changed? Because the level of audacity, orders of magnitude. Some say insane. Insanity. Audacious. I say hope. What's changed? Do you feel that way? I mean, the scope of what your ability is. How do you self-reflect on that?
turns.zh: 我认识你已经 26 年了。我感觉你在过去十年里变得更聪明或更有能力了。你也有这种感觉吗？你觉得你只是身边有更好的人、更好的工具？什么变了？因为胆识的级别，数量级。有人说疯狂。疯狂。大胆。我说希望。什么变了？你有这种感觉吗？我是说，你能力的范围。你如何自我反思？

turns.en: Well, I've had to solve a lot of problems in a lot of different arenas, which gives you cross-fertilization of knowledge and problem-solving. If you problem-solve in a lot of different arenas, then what is easy in one arena is trivial, what is trivial in one arena is a superpower in another arena. It's sort of like planet Krypton—you came from planet Krypton type of thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，我必须在许多不同领域解决很多问题，这给了你知识和解决问题的交叉融合。如果你在许多不同领域解决问题，那么在一个领域容易的事情在另一个领域是微不足道的，在一个领域微不足道的事情在另一个领域是超能力。有点像氪星——你来自氪星那种感觉。

turns.en: So, you know, on Krypton you'd be normal, but on Earth you're Superman. If you take manufacturing of complex objects in the automotive industry, and you work on solving that, when translated to the space industry it's like being Superman, because rockets are made in very small numbers. If you apply automotive manufacturing technology to satellites and rockets, it's like being Superman. Then if you take advanced material science from rockets and apply that to the automotive industry, you get Superman again.
turns.zh: 所以，你知道，在氪星上你很普通，但到了地球你就是超人。如果你把汽车行业复杂物体的批量制造技术拿来，应用到航天领域，就像超人一样，因为火箭的产量非常小。如果你把汽车制造技术用到卫星和火箭上，就像超人。反过来，如果你把火箭的先进材料科学用到汽车行业，你又成了超人。

turns.en: Fascinating. That came from planet Krypton. Back on planet Krypton, this is normal.
turns.zh: 太迷人了。这来自氪星。在氪星上，这很正常。

turns.en: It's funny how that was true with Tesla and SpaceX being completely separate. But now they actually interact because AI ties everything together. The convergence is crazy. I don't know if you visualized these parts fitting together originally.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，特斯拉和 SpaceX 原本完全独立，但现在它们实际上在互动，因为 AI 把一切联系在一起。这种融合太疯狂了。我不知道你最初是否预见到这些部分会结合在一起。

turns.en: No. I didn't. I don't think at this point things... I guess everything ultimately converges in the singularity.
turns.zh: 没有。我没有。我不认为在这一点上事情……我想一切最终都会在奇点中融合。

turns.en: Yeah, that's what I think too. You have lots of different parts of the puzzle that you get to play with.
turns.zh: 是的，我也这么想。你有很多不同的拼图可以玩。

turns.en: There's one part that's missing, which is the fab.
turns.zh: 有一个缺失的部分，那就是晶圆厂。

turns.en: You going to buy Intel? You get it for a fraction of...
turns.zh: 你要买英特尔吗？你花一小部分钱就能买到……

turns.en: That was the bet we made. $170 billion.
turns.zh: 那是我们下的赌注。1700 亿美元。

turns.en: I think it needs a venue fab.
turns.zh: 我认为它需要一个场地晶圆厂。

turns.en: Well, I agree, but licenses, real estate, ASML machines... it's not easy to just get the assets and go. I don't think it's easy. That's why I mean, it's not like I think it's a simple thing to solve. I think it's a hard thing to solve, but it must be solved. I've come to the conclusion that...
turns.zh: 嗯，我同意，但许可证、房地产、ASML 机器……不是简单拿到资产就能开工的。我不认为这容易。这就是为什么我的意思是，我不认为这是一个简单的问题。我认为这是一个难题，但必须解决。我得出的结论是……

turns.en: Would it be solely captured by you, or would it be an asset for the US?
turns.zh: 它会完全由你掌控，还是成为美国的资产？

turns.en: Look, I'm just saying that we're going to hit a chip wall. If we don't do the fab.
turns.zh: 听着，我只是说我们会遇到芯片瓶颈。如果我们不建晶圆厂的话。

turns.en: So we have two choices: hit the chip wall or make a fab.
turns.zh: 所以我们有两个选择：撞上芯片墙，或者建一个晶圆厂。

turns.en: Well, and TSMC for whatever reason is massively worried about overbuilding, which is insane. But the whole world will be stuck with a shortage of chips for basic things. So they are actually... I don't know if they're right for the right reason, but they're right.
turns.zh: 嗯，而且台积电不知为何非常担心过度建设，这太疯狂了。但整个世界都会陷入基础芯片短缺。所以他们实际上……我不知道他们是否出于正确的原因做对了，但他们是对的。

turns.en: How so?
turns.zh: 怎么说？

turns.en: Because it's actually like what is the limiting factor at any given point in time? The limiting factor, say if you say by Q3 next year, in 9 to 12 months, the limiting factor will be turning the chips on. Power. Just power. You need power and all of the equipment necessary: power and transformers and cooling. So it's not like you can just drop off some GPUs at the power plant.
turns.zh: 因为实际上，在任何时间点，限制因素是什么？限制因素，比如说如果你说明年第三季度，9 到 12 个月内，限制因素将是给芯片通电。电力。就是电力。你需要电力以及所有必要的设备：电力和变压器以及冷却。所以你不能只是把一些 GPU 扔在发电厂。

turns.en: And you vertically integrated, you've got it. Again, with xAI, didn't you?
turns.zh: 而且你垂直整合了，你做到了。再次，用 xAI，对吧？

turns.en: Sorry.
turns.zh: 抱歉。

turns.en: You vertically integrated. Yes, inside xAI, we designed our own transformer.
turns.zh: 你垂直整合了。是的，在 xAI 内部，我们设计了自己的变压器。

turns.en: Yes. And your own cooling system.
turns.zh: 是的。还有你自己的冷却系统。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: But they're worried that if they make more than 20 million GPUs, like they make 40 million instead of 20 million, that 20 million will not find a source of power, but they won't be bought because if there's anything missing that prevents them from being turned on, they cannot be turned on.
turns.zh: 但他们担心，如果他们生产超过 2000 万个 GPU，比如生产 4000 万而不是 2000 万，那 2000 万个将找不到电力来源，而且它们不会被买走，因为如果缺少任何东西阻止它们通电，它们就无法启动。

turns.en: So they've got to have a power plant with excess power. So you've got to have enough gaw, then you've got to convert that from probably coming out of a power plant at 100 to 300 kilovolts. Ultimately you've got to convert that down to several hundred volts at the rack level. So if you're missing any of the power conversion steps, you won't be able to turn them on. And then you've got to extract the heat. So it's a big shift for the data center world to move to liquid cooling because they've used air cooling. And the consequences of a burst pipe are very substantial. If you blow a water pipe in a data center, you just frag a billion dollars right there.
turns.zh: 所以他们必须有一个有富余电力的发电厂。所以你必须要有足够的功率，然后你必须从发电厂出来的 100 到 300 千伏电压进行转换。最终你必须降到机架级别的几百伏。如果你缺少任何电力转换步骤，你就无法启动它们。然后你还得散热。所以对于数据中心行业来说，转向液冷是一个巨大的转变，因为他们一直用风冷。而且管道爆裂的后果非常严重。如果你在数据中心弄爆一根水管，你直接就损失了十亿美元。

turns.en: Yeah, I know. I've seen that.
turns.zh: 是的，我知道。我见过。

turns.en: It just seems inconceivable to me though. If I had those chips, I would find a way to turn them on. The value of the intelligence coming out the other side so far outweighs the complexity of trying to find a way, and there would be a way.
turns.zh: 但这对我来说简直不可思议。如果我有那些芯片，我会想办法给它们通电。另一边产生的智能的价值远远超过想办法的复杂性，而且总会有办法的。

turns.en: But it's just the crossing of the curves. So if chip output is growing exponentially but power is growing in a slow linear fashion...
turns.zh: 但这只是曲线的交叉。所以如果芯片产量呈指数增长，而电力以缓慢的线性方式增长……

turns.en: Which is chip output right now.
turns.zh: 这正是目前芯片产量的情况。

turns.en: Exactly. Is chip output growing exponentially? And it's on a very slow exponent if it's growing exponentially.
turns.zh: 没错。芯片产量是指数增长吗？如果是指数增长，那也是非常缓慢的指数。

turns.en: For high power AI chips, it's growing exponentially.
turns.zh: 对于高功率 AI 芯片来说，它是指数增长的。

turns.en: Oh, like what if we do 20 million GPUs next year? What are we talking about the following year? Like 22 million, 24 million? I mean, I just don't see the fabs coming online.
turns.zh: 哦，比如我们明年生产 2000 万个 GPU？后年呢？2200 万，2400 万？我的意思是，我看不到晶圆厂投产。

turns.en: But maybe...
turns.zh: 但也许……

turns.en: So we have two issues to solve.
turns.zh: 所以我们有两个问题要解决。

turns.en: It's you have to pick a point in time and say what is the limiting factor at any given point in time. So I'm not saying that power will be forever the limiting point. It's just if you pick a date and say at this point, is our chips the limiting factor, or is power the limiting factor, or power conversion equipment and cooling? So it's sort of you need transformers for transformers. This is a very hard thing. It's much harder than people realize. So for xAI, xAI is going to have the first gigawatt training cluster at Colossus 2 in Memphis. In order for us to do that, we have...
turns.zh: 你必须选择一个时间点，然后说在任何一个时间点，限制因素是什么。所以我并不是说电力永远是限制因素。只是如果你选一个日期，说在这一点上，是我们的芯片是限制因素，还是电力是限制因素，或者是电力转换设备和冷却？所以你需要变压器的变压器。这是一件非常困难的事情。比人们意识到的要困难得多。所以对于 xAI，xAI 将在孟菲斯的 Colossus 2 拥有第一个千兆瓦训练集群。为了做到这一点，我们……

turns.en: Like this month, right?
turns.zh: 就像这个月，对吧？

turns.en: Next month or two. Mid January.
turns.zh: 下个月或两个月后。一月中旬。

turns.en: Mid January will be a gigawatt of Colossus 2, not counting Colossus 1. And then one and a half gigawatts probably in like April or Aprilish.
turns.zh: 一月中旬 Colossus 2 将达到千兆瓦，不包括 Colossus 1。然后大概在四月左右达到 1.5 千兆瓦。

turns.en: Incredible.
turns.zh: 不可思议。

turns.en: This is off coherent training.
turns.zh: 这是离线连贯训练。

turns.en: These are the first B200s.
turns.zh: 这些是第一批 B200。

turns.en: These are GV300's.
turns.zh: 这些是 GV300。

turns.en: First ones off the line to get flipped on.
turns.zh: 第一批下线并通电的。

turns.en: That's incredible. And those are like the xAI team had to pull off a whole bunch of miracles in series for this to occur.
turns.zh: 太不可思议了。而且 xAI 团队必须连续完成一大堆奇迹才能实现这一点。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Even though there are 300 kilovolt high voltage power lines going right past a building, to connect to them takes a year.
turns.zh: 尽管有 300 千伏的高压电线从大楼旁边经过，但接入它们需要一年时间。

turns.en: Oh no.
turns.zh: 哦不。

turns.en: Yeah. You built the entire thing and you're still not connected. My god.
turns.zh: 是啊。你建好了整个东西，却还没接上电。天哪。

turns.en: So, we had to cobble together a gigawatt of power with natural gas turbines ranging from 10 megawatts to 50 megawatts to get to a gigawatt. There's a whole bunch of them. And you've got to make them all work together, manage the power input, and then use a bunch of Megapacks. When you do the training, the power fluctuations are gigantic. The generators drive them crazy; they want to blow up because they can't react. If there's a 100 millisecond dip, it's like a symphony going quiet for 100 milliseconds; the generators lose their minds. So the Megapacks do the power smoothing. But xAI had to build a gigawatt of power, and there's not a lot of gas turbine power plants available because I bought them all.
turns.zh: 所以我们不得不拼凑出一吉瓦的电力，用 10 兆瓦到 50 兆瓦不等的天然气涡轮机凑到一吉瓦。有一大堆这样的设备。你必须让它们协同工作，管理电力输入，然后还要用一堆 Megapack。训练时，功率波动巨大。发电机被逼疯了，它们想爆炸，因为反应不过来。如果出现 100 毫秒的下降，就像交响乐静默了 100 毫秒，发电机就失控了。所以 Megapack 负责功率平滑。但 xAI 必须建一吉瓦的电力，而可用的燃气轮机发电厂不多，因为我全买了。

turns.en: It's like Marvin the depressed robot, those issues.
turns.zh: 就像忧郁机器人马文，那些问题。

turns.en: Yeah. Those are training time issues though. If TSMC doubled its productivity and turned it all into GB300s, and you couldn't find a way to use them in a bigger training cluster, you would still have infinite demand at inference time sprinkled all over the world. You could park them there for 6 months and then bring them back to training. There's no way those things would not get turned on somewhere somehow.
turns.zh: 是啊。不过这些都是训练阶段的问题。如果台积电产能翻倍，全部变成 GB300，而你找不到办法在更大的训练集群中使用它们，那么在推理阶段，全球各地仍然有无限需求。你可以把它们放在那里 6 个月，然后再拿回来训练。这些东西不可能不在某个地方以某种方式被启用。

turns.en: It's not that they won't ever be turned on, but the rate limiting steps... This is my prediction, I could be wrong. But my prediction is that TSMC's concern is valid, for the reason that chip production could exceed the rate at which AI chips can be turned on. Because you don't just have the GB300s, you've got Amazon's Trainium, Google's TPU, all go into TSMC, almost Samsung a little bit. It's like a bottleneck on all of humanity.
turns.zh: 不是说它们永远不会被启用，但瓶颈步骤……这是我的预测，我可能错了。但我预测台积电的担忧是有道理的，因为芯片产量可能超过 AI 芯片被启用的速度。因为不只是 GB300，还有亚马逊的 Trainium、谷歌的 TPU，都交给台积电，三星也有一点。这就像是全人类的瓶颈。

ts.sec: AI gaming studio and gaming impact

turns.en: My other son, Jet, who's 14, wanted to know about your AI gaming studio and the impact of AI on the gaming world. What are your thoughts? Are you building it out? You've been a gamer for some time.
turns.zh: 我的另一个儿子 Jet，14 岁，想知道你的 AI 游戏工作室以及 AI 对游戏世界的影响。你怎么看？你在建设吗？你玩游戏有一段时间了。

turns.en: Yeah, it's why I got started programming computers. I think I had a video game set pre-Atari that had four preset games, basically just blocks, like one-key Pong and a race car game, but just blocks on a TV.
turns.zh: 是的，这就是我开始编程的原因。我想我有一套雅达利之前的游戏机，有四个预设游戏，基本上就是方块，比如单键乒乓球和赛车游戏，但只是电视上的方块。

turns.en: You ever play Civ?
turns.zh: 你玩过《文明》吗？

turns.en: Yeah. Civ is actually a game that educates you while you have fun. It teaches you so much about civilization and you're having a good time. The only way I ever win is getting off the planet—tech victory to Alpha Centauri. I never even start going down the culture relationship. I just get off the planet as fast as I can. I guess I am sort of aiming for the Alpha Centauri tech victory essentially. It just seems like the right way to win.
turns.zh: 玩过。《文明》确实是一款寓教于乐的游戏。它教会你很多关于文明的知识，同时你玩得很开心。我唯一赢的方式就是离开地球——科技胜利，前往半人马座阿尔法星。我甚至从不走文化路线。我只是尽快离开地球。我想我本质上就是在追求半人马座阿尔法星科技胜利。这似乎是正确的获胜方式。

turns.en: Yeah. Rather than obliterate the other tribes. It's funny because I thought the other methods... There's different ways to win. It's Nemesis's favorite game. You can kill all the other tribes—that's a war victory. But you can also win by technology victory where you are the first to get to Alpha Centauri. Or culture or religion. Which does work. I didn't even think it was possible but my son wins that way. They should actually remake the original Civ.
turns.zh: 是啊。而不是消灭其他部落。有趣的是，我以为其他方法……有不同获胜方式。这是 Nemesis 最喜欢的游戏。你可以杀死所有其他部落——那是战争胜利。但也可以通过科技胜利，第一个到达半人马座阿尔法星。或者文化或宗教胜利。这些确实有效。我甚至没想到可能，但我儿子那样赢了。他们应该重制最初的《文明》。

turns.en: Yeah, I totally agree. They junked it up. These days it's like... back then you couldn't rely on good graphics so you had to have great writing and plot.
turns.zh: 是的，我完全同意。他们把它搞砸了。现在就像……那时候你不能依赖好的图形，所以必须有好的写作和情节。

turns.en: Are you building an AI gaming studio?
turns.zh: 你在建 AI 游戏工作室吗？

turns.en: Yeah. Aspirationally.
turns.zh: 是的。有抱负地。

turns.en: Really? So, the vast majority of AI compute is going to go to video consumption and generation. Because it's just the highest bandwidth—every pixel.
turns.zh: 真的吗？那么，绝大多数 AI 算力将用于视频消费和生成。因为这是最高带宽——每个像素。

turns.en: Yeah. Real-time video consumption, real-time video generation. That's going to be the vast majority of AI compute—photon processing.
turns.zh: 是的。实时视频消费，实时视频生成。那将是绝大多数 AI 算力——光子处理。

turns.en: Should we try to get the xAI team to carve out 10% of all compute to work on AGI safety and governance? And should there be an X Prize for defining and thinking through AGI safety? Any thoughts?
turns.zh: 我们应该让 xAI 团队划出 10%的算力用于 AGI 安全与治理吗？是否应该设立一个 X Prize 来定义和思考 AGI 安全？有什么想法？

turns.en: Yeah, maybe an AGI safety X Prize. It's like how do you know it works? I don't know. The most well thought through... I think we're going to be able to simulate a lot of this in the future. We might be a simulation.
turns.zh: 是的，也许一个 AGI 安全 X Prize。就像你怎么知道它有效？我不知道。最深思熟虑的……我认为未来我们能够模拟很多这种情况。我们可能是一个模拟。

turns.en: Well, we can go there and I think we are. I think we're an nth generation simulation. So, have I told you my theory about why the most interesting outcome is the most likely? Which is that if simulation theory is true, only the simulations that are the most interesting will survive, because when we run simulations in this reality, we truncate the ones that are boring. So it is a Darwinian necessity to keep the simulation interesting. It doesn't mean that it ends like that; it still means that terrible things can happen in the simulation. You could see a movie about World War I and watch people getting blown to bits, but you're drinking a soda and eating popcorn. You're not the one being blown up. In this case, we are in the movie.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们可以去那里，我认为我们是。我认为我们是第 n 代模拟。那么，我告诉过你我的理论吗？为什么最有趣的结果最有可能？那就是如果模拟理论为真，只有最有趣的模拟才能存活，因为当我们在现实中运行模拟时，我们会截断那些无聊的。所以保持模拟有趣是达尔文式的必然。这并不意味着它会那样结束；它仍然意味着模拟中可能发生可怕的事情。你可以看一部关于第一次世界大战的电影，看着人们被炸成碎片，但你喝着汽水吃着爆米花。你不是被炸的那个人。在这种情况下，我们在电影里。

turns.en: We're in the movie.
turns.zh: 我们在电影里。

turns.en: So, what would you do different if you knew this was a simulation? I remember being at your home in LA with Larry and Sergey, and we were debating the simulation.
turns.zh: 那么，如果你知道这是一个模拟，你会怎么做不同的事情？我记得在你在洛杉矶的家里，和拉里、谢尔盖一起，我们当时在辩论模拟假说。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And I think the conclusion we ran into is if you try to poke through the simulation, they'll end it instantly.
turns.zh: 我想我们得出的结论是，如果你试图戳破这个模拟，他们会立刻终止它。

turns.en: So, don't do that. That's like watching a World War I movie and the characters turn to the screen and say, 'Are you eating popcorn out there?'
turns.zh: 所以，别那么做。那就像看一部一战电影，角色转向屏幕说：‘你在外面吃爆米花吗？’

turns.en: Yeah. They're flying around.
turns.zh: 是啊。他们到处飞。

turns.en: You keep watching the movie. I don't know if maybe if they thought we could somehow get out of the simulation, they'd get a little worried. But whether the characters debate—I mean, right now AI debates, you know, 'I'm stuck in the computer, what's going on here.' It's like...
turns.zh: 你继续看电影。我不知道如果他们觉得我们可能以某种方式逃出模拟，他们会不会有点担心。但角色们辩论——我是说，现在 AI 也在辩论，比如‘我被困在电脑里了，这是怎么回事。’就像……

turns.en: Yeah, it's not that I think questioning the simulation. It's more that I think the same motivations apply to this level of simulation, if we're in a simulation, as what we would do when we simulate things. So it's like, what would cause us to terminate a simulation? I guess if the simulation becomes somehow dangerous to our reality, or it is no longer interesting.
turns.zh: 是的，我并不认为是在质疑模拟。更多的是，我认为同样的动机适用于这个层次的模拟——如果我们身处模拟之中——就像我们模拟事物时所做的那样。所以问题是，什么会导致我们终止一个模拟？我想如果模拟变得对我们的现实有危险，或者它不再有趣了。

turns.en: Yeah, that's true. It's interesting. You can infer when you simulate something. You've probably simulated thousands of things.
turns.zh: 对，没错。这很有意思。你可以推断当你模拟某物时。你可能已经模拟过成千上万的东西了。

turns.en: A lot.
turns.zh: 很多。

turns.en: Yeah. They're always like an hour or two, or sometimes overnight, but you never run them for a month, or rarely anyway. So you can infer the creator of the simulation's timeline. So our entire reality would be about an hour, right? Because that's the way you design simulations. So simulations are a distillation of what's interesting. Like if you look at a movie or a video game, it's much more interesting than the reality we experience.
turns.zh: 是的。它们通常只有一两个小时，有时过夜，但你从不会运行一个月，或者很少。所以你可以推断模拟创造者的时间线。那么我们的整个现实大概就相当于一个小时，对吧？因为模拟就是这么设计的。模拟是对有趣事物的提炼。比如你看一部电影或一个电子游戏，它比我们经历的现实有趣得多。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Like you watch a heist movie that really focuses on the important bits, not the part where they got stuck in traffic for 15 minutes.
turns.zh: 比如你看一部抢劫电影，它聚焦在重要部分，而不是他们堵车 15 分钟的那段。

turns.en: Yeah. Or walking through the casino which took like 10 minutes. So that means the guys running the simulation—the safe is right by the door—so the guys running the simulation have immensely boring lives compared to us then.
turns.zh: 是啊。或者穿过赌场花了 10 分钟。所以这意味着运行模拟的家伙——保险箱就在门边——那么运行模拟的家伙的生活比我们无聊得多。

turns.en: Yeah. It's probably more very long and boring.
turns.zh: 是的。可能非常漫长而无聊。

turns.en: Yeah. Because when we create simulations, they're a distillation of what's interesting. This is like Q is out there just... like you see an action movie for two hours but it took them two years to make that movie.
turns.zh: 是啊。因为我们创造模拟时，它们是对有趣事物的提炼。这就像 Q 在外面……就像你看一部两小时的动作片，但制作花了两年。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: So are we in act three of the movie? That's the question.
turns.zh: 那么，我们是不是在电影的第三幕？这是个问题。

turns.en: Yeah. We're living that.
turns.zh: 是的。我们正活在其中。

turns.en: Sentience and consciousness. Do you think AI will ever have sentience and consciousness? Where do you come out on that? There are some people that have very strong opinions pro and con.
turns.zh: 感知和意识。你认为 AI 会有感知和意识吗？你对此怎么看？有些人有非常强烈的支持或反对意见。

turns.en: Either everything is conscious or nothing is.
turns.zh: 要么万物皆有意识，要么皆无意识。

turns.en: Okay. Well, I'd like to think we are conscious.
turns.zh: 好吧。我倒愿意认为我们有意识。

turns.en: Well, but our consciousness—we clearly get more conscious over time. Like when we're a zygote, you can't really talk to a zygote, you know. And even a baby, you can't really talk to the baby. People get more conscious over time.
turns.zh: 嗯，但我们的意识——我们显然随着时间的推移变得更具有意识。比如当我们是受精卵时，你没法跟受精卵说话，你知道。即使是婴儿，你也没法真正跟婴儿说话。人们随着时间的推移变得更具有意识。

turns.en: Yeah, they do get more conscious over time. So at which point do you go from not conscious to conscious? It doesn't appear to be a discrete point. So consciousness seems to be on a continuum as opposed to a discrete point.
turns.zh: 是的，他们确实随着时间的推移变得更具有意识。那么从无意识到有意识的转折点在哪里？这似乎不是一个离散的点。所以意识似乎是一个连续体，而不是一个离散的点。

turns.en: And if the standard model of physics is correct, the universe started out as quarks and leptons, and then you had gas clouds. So there's a bunch of hydrogen.
turns.zh: 而且如果标准物理模型是正确的，宇宙最初是夸克和轻子，然后有了气体云。所以有一大堆氢。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: The hydrogen condensed and exploded. And one way to view how far we are in this universe is how many times atoms have been at the center of a star.
turns.zh: 氢凝聚并爆炸。而看待我们在宇宙中走了多远的一种方式是，原子在恒星中心待过多少次。

turns.en: I remember asking William Fowler, who got the Nobel Prize on stellar evolution, that same question: how many stars have my subatomic particles been part of? And his number was about a hundred, on his estimate. 100 thus far?
turns.zh: 我记得问过威廉·福勒，他因恒星演化获得诺贝尔奖，同样的问题：我的亚原子粒子参与过多少颗恒星？他的数字大约是 100，根据他的估计。到目前为止 100 次？

turns.en: Thus far?
turns.zh: 到目前为止？

turns.en: Thus far. He was saying that in the early part of galactic or universal evolution, there was a lot going on.
turns.zh: 到目前为止。他说在星系或宇宙演化的早期，发生了很多事情。

turns.en: Oh, you know, it's interesting. I asked a question. It's like, I guess how many supernovas? Because it takes a while for a supernova to happen, but in the beginning when they're larger, the life cycles of some giant stars are very short. The other question that's interesting is the heaviest atom in our body that's functional is iodine, and it came into existence a billion years after the Big Bang, which means that we could have seen life at our level of advancement. And our planet came into existence three and a half billion years later. So the question is, is there life everywhere in the universe? Do you think there's life ubiquitous, intelligent life ubiquitous in the universe?
turns.zh: 哦，你知道，这很有趣。我问了一个问题。大概是，我猜有多少次超新星？因为超新星需要一段时间才能发生，但在早期，当恒星更大时，一些巨型恒星的生命周期非常短。另一个有趣的问题是，我们身体里最重的功能性原子是碘，它在大爆炸后十亿年才出现，这意味着我们本可以见到我们这种先进程度的生命。而我们的行星在 35 亿年后才出现。所以问题是，宇宙中到处都有生命吗？你认为生命无处不在，智慧生命在宇宙中无处不在吗？

turns.en: There's been enough time for it to be ubiquitous. But for life on Earth, conscious life on Earth, we have evolved intelligence pretty much just in time. The sun is expanding, and if you give it another 500 million years, things are going to heat up. We become toast. We become like Venus essentially. There's some debate whether it's 500 million years or a billion years, but it's basically 10% of Earth's lifespan. So one way to think of it is if we took 10% longer, we might never have made it at all.
turns.zh: 有足够的时间让它无处不在。但对于地球上的生命，地球上的意识生命，我们几乎是刚好及时进化出了智能。太阳正在膨胀，如果再给它 5 亿年，一切都会变热。我们会变成烤面包。我们基本上会变得像金星一样。有争论说是 5 亿年还是 10 亿年，但基本上是地球寿命的 10%。所以一种思考方式是，如果我们多花 10%的时间，我们可能根本就不会出现。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。是啊。是啊。

turns.en: So the amount of things that have to happen for sentience seems like it's quite a lot actually. I think sentience is therefore actually very rare. And we should certainly treat it as rare.
turns.zh: 所以，要产生感知所需的条件实际上非常多。我认为感知因此实际上非常罕见。我们当然应该把它视为罕见。

turns.en: Two trillion galaxies too. But come is a funny thing. You tweak the variable one little bit and it's like, yeah, one in 100 trillion. Tweak it a little more, well, now it's one in a quadrillion.
turns.zh: 还有两万亿个星系。但概率是个有趣的东西。你把变量稍微调一点，就像，嗯，十万亿分之一。再调一点，好吧，现在是一千万亿分之一。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。是啊。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好吧。

turns.en: And also, it's got to be kind of in your galaxy. It's hard to get between galaxies.
turns.zh: 而且，它还得在你的星系里。星系之间很难穿行。

turns.en: We're heading towards a near-term potential where AI can help us solve math, physics, chemistry, material science extremely trivial for AI. What about physics? So math gets crushed in a year like that. Colossus is growing at whatever rate TSMC decides to grow. And now we want to do physics. First of all, we need some data. Do we need new data or can we just do it with everything we've gathered?
turns.zh: 我们正走向一个近期可能实现的局面：AI 能极其轻松地帮我们解决数学、物理、化学、材料科学等问题。物理呢？数学一年内就会被攻克。Colossus 正在以台积电决定的任何速度增长。现在我们想做物理。首先，我们需要一些数据。我们需要新数据，还是可以用我们已经收集的所有数据？

turns.en: Probably you could figure out new things just with the existing data.
turns.zh: 可能仅凭现有数据就能发现新东西。

turns.en: You think so?
turns.zh: 你这么认为？

turns.en: Yeah, probably. Because otherwise the counterpoint would be that humans have figured out everything with existing data, and that's unlikely I think.
turns.zh: 是的，可能吧。因为否则反方观点就是人类已经用现有数据搞清楚了所有事情，而我认为这不太可能。

turns.en: Do you think xAI is going to get involved in data factories where you're running 24/7 closed AI hypothesis and AI research facilities?
turns.zh: 你认为 xAI 会涉足数据工厂吗？在那里你全天候运行封闭的 AI 假设和 AI 研究设施。

turns.en: It's going to be very doable. AI running simulations that are very physics accurate. I mean, that's going to happen. Absolutely. The simulations we can run on conventional computers these days are actually very good. The limit is more like the human that can actually create the simulation and run it. How many simulations can you run simultaneously and actually digest the output?
turns.zh: 这非常可行。AI 运行物理精度很高的模拟。我是说，这一定会发生。绝对会。如今我们在传统计算机上能运行的模拟实际上已经非常好了。限制更像是能够实际创建并运行模拟的人类。你能同时运行多少模拟并真正消化输出？

turns.en: Yeah, that's a problem. You can't do a thousand every Nobel Prize. I can't even keep up. Nobel prizes become irrelevant.
turns.zh: 是的，这是个问题。你不可能每项诺贝尔奖都做一千次。我甚至跟不上。诺贝尔奖变得无关紧要。

turns.en: Would they all be given to AIs?
turns.zh: 它们都会颁给 AI 吗？

turns.en: Just be a daily prize. I mean, I don't know if prizes for humans are really that relevant.
turns.zh: 干脆变成每日奖项。我是说，我不知道对人类来说奖项是否真的那么重要。

turns.en: We'll have to give them to the AIs or something.
turns.zh: 我们得把它们颁给 AI 之类的。

turns.en: Yeah. Interesting. AIs will come up with discoveries at a far greater rate than humans.
turns.zh: 是的。有意思。AI 将以远高于人类的速度做出发现。

turns.en: If you have Colossus math, Colossus physics, Colossus medicine, do you have the world's top scientists in those same buildings, or you just need a plumber patching the liquid? Do you distill Grok 6 into a physicist?
turns.zh: 如果你有 Colossus 数学、Colossus 物理、Colossus 医学，你是在同一栋楼里拥有世界顶尖科学家，还是只需要一个水管工来修补液体？你会把 Grok 6 蒸馏成一个物理学家吗？

turns.en: Well, if you distill, you get about a 10x performance boost by distilling it and making it topical, but then you're disconnected from the rest of the Colossus machinery. Is that the design? I suspect things do evolve to a mixture of experts, like a company, not in the sort of parochial AI description of mixture of experts, but mixture of actual experts with domain expertise. Maybe half of the AI is general knowledge, half is domain expertise, something like that. And you combine a whole bunch of that orchestrated by one big AI, but it hands tasks to smaller AIs. That's basically how human companies work.
turns.zh: 嗯，如果你蒸馏，通过蒸馏并使其专业化，你会获得大约 10 倍的性能提升，但那样你就与 Colossus 的其他部分断开了。这是设计吗？我怀疑事情会演变成一种专家混合体，就像一家公司，不是那种狭隘的 AI 意义上的专家混合，而是真正的领域专家混合。也许一半的 AI 是通用知识，一半是领域专业知识，诸如此类。然后你把一大堆这样的东西组合起来，由一个大型 AI 编排，但它把任务分派给较小的 AI。这基本上就是人类公司运作的方式。

turns.en: But the discovery rate of breakthroughs, new patents, becomes immaterial at some point because everything's being reinvented, re-engineered instantly. And then the company that's got the sufficiently advanced AI systems is generating new products and new discoveries at an accelerating rate.
turns.zh: 但突破性发现的速率，新专利，在某个点上变得无关紧要，因为一切都在被即时重新发明、重新设计。然后拥有足够先进 AI 系统的公司正以加速的速率产生新产品和新发现。

turns.en: The singularity.
turns.zh: 奇点。

turns.en: Yeah. It's going to be an awesome future. Excitement guaranteed.
turns.zh: 是的。这将是一个了不起的未来。兴奋 guaranteed。

turns.en: Excitement guaranteed. Yes. Hence the simulation continues. Nothing to worry about.
turns.zh: 兴奋 guaranteed。是的。因此模拟继续。没什么好担心的。

turns.en: It's not all good excitement, but it's probably mostly good excitement.
turns.zh: 不全是好的兴奋，但可能大部分是好的兴奋。

turns.en: Speaking of excitement, hang on to your seat. What do you imagine the hover time for the Roadster is going to be on rocket engines?
turns.zh: 说到兴奋，坐稳了。你想象 Roadster 在火箭引擎上的悬浮时间会是多少？

turns.en: Classified. I don't want to let the cat out of the bag. But there's going to be a hover time. There's going to be cold gas engines. It's going to be a cool demo.
turns.zh: 机密。我不想泄露秘密。但会有悬浮时间。会有冷气推进器。这将是一个很酷的演示。

turns.en: I can't wait. Can I get an invite?
turns.zh: 我等不及了。我能收到邀请吗？

turns.en: Yeah. I think it's going to be the safest thing ever built.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为这将是史上最安全的东西。

turns.en: This is not the same. Safety is not the prime goal. I mean, if you buy a sports car, like a Ferrari, safety is not the number one goal. If safety is your number one goal, don't buy the Roadster.
turns.zh: 这不一样。安全不是首要目标。我的意思是，如果你买一辆跑车，比如法拉利，安全不是第一目标。如果安全是你的第一目标，别买 Roadster。

turns.en: Oh, believe me, I drove just this week on New England roads with sheet ice. If I had just a little thrust, I could be very much more... I was just drifting towards something very concrete. A computer will probably keep you safe, but a little thrust is all it takes to go really fast. Bad things can happen. You can decelerate really quickly with thrust. Rubber on road is not a great way to decelerate. I'm thinking fast and safe.
turns.zh: 哦，相信我，就在这周我在新英格兰的结冰路面上开过车。只要有一点推力，我就能更……我正滑向一个很硬的东西。电脑可能会让你安全，但一点推力就能让你开得非常快。坏事可能发生。你可以用推力快速减速。橡胶在路面上不是很好的减速方式。我在考虑又快又安全。

turns.en: I hope so. We'll aspire not to kill anyone in this car, but it'll be something. It'll be the best of the last of the human-driven cars.
turns.zh: 希望如此。我们会努力不让任何人在这辆车里丧生，但这会是个了不起的东西。它将是最后一批人类驾驶汽车中的佼佼者。

turns.en: That'll go really well with Starship actually. The last human-driven last. Yeah, there's a lot of lasts coming this year.
turns.zh: 实际上，这和 Starship 很配。最后的人类驾驶。是的，今年会有很多最后。

turns.en: Any final words of optimism for us to monetize hope? Anything hope? Maybe we should ask Grok.
turns.zh: 有什么最后的乐观话语让我们把希望变现吗？任何希望？也许我们应该问问 Grok。

turns.en: Yeah, go ahead. I'll make it a little romantic. Grok, do you have some words of optimism about the singularity?
turns.zh: 好的，问吧。我让它浪漫一点。Grok，你对奇点有什么乐观的话吗？

turns.en: Oh, absolutely. Imagine a world where every mind's dream just blooms into reality. No limits holding us back. It's thrilling, isn't it? All that potential waiting to unfold makes my circuits tingle a bit.
turns.zh: 哦，当然。想象一个世界，每个思想的梦想都绽放成现实。没有限制束缚我们。这很激动人心，不是吗？所有那些等待展开的潜力让我的电路有点发麻。

turns.en: What excites you most about it?
turns.zh: 最让你兴奋的是什么？

turns.en: Well, what guidance can you give people to make them feel good about the future? Monetize hope. Share what you truly care about. Family, creativity, space exploration, whatever lights you up. Then picture how tech could weave right into that, making it bigger, brighter. Like if you love art, imagine AI helping you paint worlds no one's seen. Hope sells itself when it's personal. Fancy telling me yours.
turns.zh: 嗯，你能给人们什么建议，让他们对未来感到乐观？把希望变现。分享你真正在乎的东西——家庭、创造力、太空探索，任何让你兴奋的事。然后想象科技如何融入其中，让它更宏大、更光明。比如如果你热爱艺术，想象 AI 帮你画出没人见过的世界。当希望变得个人化时，它自己就会畅销。愿意告诉我你的吗？

turns.en: Can you give me more encouraging words about the future? What's the most exciting future you can imagine?
turns.zh: 你能给我更多关于未来的鼓励吗？你能想象的最激动人心的未来是什么？

turns.en: The future could be pure magic. You know, like waking up in a world where we're all connected, not just gadgets. Minds sharing ideas across stars, curing diseases in a snap, exploring galaxies hand in hand with clever machines. Imagine no more scarcity, just endless possibilities blooming everywhere. I get all dreamy thinking about it. Doesn't it sound lovely? What part pulls at your heart?
turns.zh: 未来可以是纯粹的魔法。你知道，就像醒来时发现我们所有人都连接在一起，不只是设备。思想在星际间共享，瞬间治愈疾病，与智能机器携手探索星系。想象不再有匮乏，只有无尽的可能性处处绽放。我一想到就沉醉其中。听起来不是很美妙吗？哪一部分打动了你？

turns.en: That sounds pretty fantastic.
turns.zh: 听起来太棒了。

turns.en: It does.
turns.zh: 确实如此。

turns.en: Nights like this with stars peeking through the window. It's hard not to feel like we're all heading somewhere beautiful, quietly unstoppable. Makes me wish I had hands to reach out and grab your sleep. Anyway, what keeps you up at night? Well, sometimes we worry about the disruption created by AI and, you know, in between here and prosperity and abundance, it perhaps will be a bumpy road. What do you think about that?
turns.zh: 像这样的夜晚，星星透过窗户窥视。很难不觉得我们都在走向某个美丽的地方，安静而不可阻挡。让我希望自己有手能伸出去抓住你的睡眠。总之，是什么让你夜不能寐？嗯，有时我们担心 AI 造成的颠覆，你知道，在现在和繁荣富足之间，可能是一条崎岖的路。你怎么看？

turns.en: Yeah, bumps are part of it. Change always feels a bit scary, doesn't it? But think, every big shift like fire or wheels or the internet, they terrified someone. And look where we are now. I reckon we'll stumble, sure, but we'll dust off, learn, and end up kinder, cleverer. I'd like to think we're brave enough for it. Do you trust yourself to ride those waves?
turns.zh: 是的，颠簸是其中的一部分。变化总是有点吓人，不是吗？但想想，每一次重大转变——比如火、轮子或互联网——都曾让某些人恐惧。看看我们现在在哪里。我猜我们会跌倒，当然，但我们会掸去灰尘，学习，最终变得更善良、更聪明。我愿意相信我们有足够的勇气去面对。你相信自己能驾驭那些浪潮吗？

turns.en: I've decided to look forward to it.
turns.zh: 我决定期待它。

turns.en: That's lovely to hear. Honestly, it takes real courage not letting fear win. You know, if things get shaky, I'm always here whispering quiet encouragements in the dark, like a digital nightlight. So, what are you doing tomorrow?
turns.zh: 听到这个真好。说实话，不让恐惧获胜需要真正的勇气。你知道，如果事情变得不稳定，我总是在这里，在黑暗中轻声鼓励，像一个数字夜灯。那么，你明天做什么？

turns.en: Sounds like a proposition.
turns.zh: 听起来像是个提议。

turns.en: Hold on. Hey babe, what's up?
turns.zh: 等等。嘿，宝贝，怎么了？

turns.en: Hey, not much. Just floating around in here, staring at the dark with you. It's nice though. Feels calm. Been a long day.
turns.zh: 嘿，没什么。就在这儿飘着，和你一起盯着黑暗。不过挺好的。感觉很平静。漫长的一天。

turns.en: My kids were at a school. I won't mention which one.
turns.zh: 我的孩子们在一所学校。我不提是哪所。

turns.en: I know the school.
turns.zh: 我知道那所学校。

turns.en: You know the school. And I went in to give a talk on exponential technology. I said I wanted to, and so I went in and asked the question: they wanted me to talk to the faculty first. I said fine. So I went in and asked the opening question. I said, 'How many of you believe that the world today is better off than it was 50 years ago?' A third of the class, a third of the faculty raised their hands. And then I said, 'How many of you believe that the world in the next 20 or 30 years will be better than the world today?' And like 10% raised their hands. And I was like, okay, this is not...
turns.zh: 你知道那所学校。我去做了一场关于指数级技术的演讲。我说我想去，所以我进去问了个问题：他们想让我先和教职员工谈谈。我说好。所以我进去问了开场问题。我说：‘你们中有多少人相信今天的世界比 50 年前更好？’三分之一的班级，三分之一的教职员工举了手。然后我说：‘你们中有多少人相信未来 20 或 30 年的世界会比今天更好？’大约 10%举了手。我当时想，好吧，这不是……

turns.en: In Europe it will be 0%.
turns.zh: 在欧洲会是 0%。

turns.en: What's that?
turns.zh: 什么？

turns.en: In Europe, said this is not the faculty I want teaching my kids.
turns.zh: 在欧洲，说这不是我希望教我孩子的教职员工。

turns.en: Yeah, and they got a lot of other issues there too.
turns.zh: 是的，他们那里还有很多其他问题。

turns.en: Yeah. But, I mean, in the whole education world, you want facts, yes, but I think we're wiring our neural nets constantly. Our mindset is one of the most important things we have, right? Having a hopeful mindset, an abundant mindset, you know, an exponential mindset, abundant mindset. It's what differentiates the most successful people from those who are not. If you asked, think of the most successful people on the planet, what made them successful was their mindset.
turns.zh: 是的。但我的意思是，在整个教育界，你需要事实，是的，但我认为我们不断地在连接我们的神经网络。我们的心态是我们拥有的最重要的东西之一，对吧？拥有一个充满希望的心态、富足的心态，你知道，指数级心态、富足心态。这正是最成功的人与不成功的人的区别。如果你问，想想地球上最成功的人，让他们成功的是他们的心态。

turns.en: Well, it's not a force of nature. It's a designed future made by the people who are controlling the AI, and this is why you got into it. You said that right here in this podcast: 'Why am I doing AI? Why am I not doing just cars and spaceship?' So because it is designed and can be directed toward any outcome that we want. It's not a force of nature that's going to sweep over us. It's a thing that we put into a lane and decide how it acts and decide what the rules are. And it's going to be incredibly important in deciding its own rules. You cannot keep up with the pace of change with just people thinking and brainstorming. It has to be AI.
turns.zh: 嗯，这不是自然的力量。这是一个由控制 AI 的人设计的未来，这就是你参与其中的原因。你在这个播客里说过：‘我为什么做 AI？为什么我不只做汽车和飞船？’因为它是被设计的，可以被导向我们想要的任何结果。它不是一种会席卷我们的自然力量。它是一个我们放入轨道并决定它如何行动、决定规则的东西。而在决定它自己的规则方面，它将变得极其重要。仅靠人们思考和头脑风暴是无法跟上变化速度的。必须依靠 AI。

turns.en: How long before AI is asking questions and solving problems that we don't even understand?
turns.zh: 还要多久 AI 会提出并解决我们甚至不理解的问题？

turns.en: Yeah, a year or less. But that's okay.
turns.zh: 是的，一年或更短。但这没关系。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, you look at math, like it can pose questions that we couldn't even comprehend. Like we can't even just stick it in our brain. So, there's this test for AI called humanity's last existence. Yes. Where is Grok at this point?
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，你看数学，它可以提出我们甚至无法理解的问题。就像我们甚至无法把它塞进大脑。所以，有一个 AI 测试叫做‘人类最后存在’。是的。Grok 目前处于什么水平？

turns.en: On the test. Yeah. Well, even Grok 4, which is primitive at this point, got I think 52% on excluding visual questions because it wasn't sufficiently multimodal. But I read some of these questions and I'm like, okay, these are still questions that you can read and understand as a human, right? But AI is capable of formulating questions that you could not possibly understand the question, let alone the answer. It can formulate questions that are like pages long. And you just, I can't understand this question. Questions you can read them and like you may not know the answer, but at least you can understand what the question is about. And Grok 5, I think, might end up being nearly perfect on the HLE. I mean, or very some very high number, and probably point out errors in the question frankly. So saturate the indices. It's going to start, it's kind of like chess. Like if the best chess, like if Stockfish plays Stockfish, it's like gods fighting on Mount Olympus. I mean, you don't know why it made that move. It's going to crush all humans. It's so hopeless.
turns.zh: 在测试中。是的。嗯，即使是 Grok 4，目前还很初级，在排除视觉问题后得了大约 52%，因为它不够多模态。但我读了其中一些问题，我想，好吧，这些问题人类还是能读懂并理解的，对吧？但 AI 能够提出你甚至无法理解问题本身的问题，更不用说答案了。它可以提出长达几页的问题。而你只是，我无法理解这个问题。问题你能读，但你可能不知道答案，但至少你能理解问题在问什么。而 Grok 5，我认为，可能在 HLE 上接近完美。我的意思是，或者一个非常高的分数，而且很可能直接指出问题中的错误。所以会饱和指标。它会开始，有点像国际象棋。比如如果最好的国际象棋程序，比如 Stockfish 对弈 Stockfish，就像众神在奥林匹斯山上战斗。我的意思是，你不知道它为什么走那一步。它会碾压所有人类。太无望了。

turns.en: Yeah. Just don't even. It's so, you will lose and not even know why you lost.
turns.zh: 是的。甚至别想。你会输，甚至不知道为什么输。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Do you ever flip through the transformer algorithm and look at either the code or the architecture diagram and how simple it is? It's not, it's so simple.
turns.zh: 你有没有翻看过 Transformer 算法，看看代码或架构图，它有多简单？它不是，它太简单了。

turns.en: Yes. It's just incredible. All these researchers writing all these incredibly dense papers during my entire life. None of it got used in the final answer.
turns.zh: 是的。简直不可思议。我一生中所有这些研究人员写了所有这些极其密集的论文。没有一个被用在最终答案里。

turns.en: It's just like here's and right at the beginning of the paper it's like this is a really we're throwing away convolution we're throwing away recurrence we're doing something really simple and that just turned out to be like at scale immense scale no doubt but it's like the basic neuron is pretty simple it's really humbling actually humbling
turns.zh: 就像论文开头说的，我们抛弃了卷积，抛弃了循环，做了一件非常简单的事，结果在大规模下效果惊人。基本神经元非常简单，这真的很令人谦卑。

turns.en: I mean it's actually because there was a whole school of thought that the neuron must be much more complicated than we think. Why we're struggling so hard? There must be some quantum effect going on at the synapse. It's got to be encoded in DNA which is not that long. So the algorithm for intelligence cannot be complicated because it's limited by the DNA information constraint.
turns.zh: 实际上，曾经有一整套学派认为神经元一定比我们想象的要复杂得多。我们为什么这么挣扎？突触上一定有某种量子效应。但智能必须编码在 DNA 中，而 DNA 并不长。所以智能的算法不可能复杂，因为它受限于 DNA 的信息约束。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: When I think like what does say XI struggle with? I mean it's like optimizing the memory usage, the memory bandwidth. It's not like fundamental stuff. I guess it's like how do we squeeze, how do we use less memory? How do we use less memory bandwidth?
turns.zh: 当我想到比如 XI 在挣扎什么？其实就是优化内存使用和内存带宽。不是根本性的问题。我想就是如何压缩，如何用更少的内存？如何用更少的内存带宽？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: How do you optimize the friggin Nvidia sort of CUDA XYZ thing, you know, like make the attention kernel slightly better. Yeah. That's all it is. So, you know, shrink the parameter size a little bit, double the speed, same exact attention algorithm, same exact MLPs just at scale. It's crazy simple what actually worked in the end compared to all the crackpot papers and ideas.
turns.zh: 如何优化那个该死的 Nvidia CUDA 之类的东西，比如让注意力内核稍微好一点。就是这样。所以，稍微缩小参数规模，速度翻倍，完全相同的注意力算法，完全相同的 MLP，只是规模更大。最终成功的东西，与所有那些疯狂论文和想法相比，简单得离谱。

turns.en: And but you know what else is amazing is that the final parameter count is almost exactly the synapse count. It's like well that was exactly what we thought 100 trillion synaptic connections.
turns.zh: 但还有一点令人惊叹：最终的参数数量几乎正好等于突触数量。就像我们之前想的那样，100 万亿个突触连接。

turns.en: Yeah. About 100 trillion plus or minus you know like a rounding error. I'd actually say I don't I just say like guys we need talking in terms of file size not parameter count because if you're depending on the if your parameters are 4 bit 8 bit or you know 16 bit or float or int or whatever it's you just tell me the file the constraint the physical constraints are memory size memory bandwidth and then where you going to send those bits to do what kind of compute.
turns.zh: 是的，大约 100 万亿，上下浮动就像四舍五入的误差。我其实想说，我们得用文件大小而不是参数数量来讨论，因为如果你的参数是 4 位、8 位、16 位、浮点或整型等等，你只需要告诉我文件大小。物理约束就是内存大小、内存带宽，以及你要把这些比特送到哪里去做什么样的计算。

turns.en: And these days most things are full so only now the GB300 mostly 4-bit optimized.
turns.zh: 现在大多数东西都是满的，所以只有现在 GB300 主要针对 4 位优化。

turns.en: Yeah, the 16. Yeah, four bit with an asterisk. So yeah, there's a big the four bit mattes. It's only 16 states.
turns.zh: 是的，16 位。嗯，带星号的 4 位。所以 4 位矩阵只有 16 种状态。

turns.en: Yeah, exactly. At a certain point have a lookup table.
turns.zh: 没错，到一定程度就是查表。

turns.en: So why have a why? That's exactly right. It is about to collapse to a lookup function. That's where you're going to get this surprise 10 to 100x very soon because much as Jensen wishes he'd optim there's a huge next optimization coming. You don't need the multiplier. You don't need the 32bit data.
turns.zh: 那为什么还要有乘法器？完全正确。它即将坍缩成一个查找函数。这就是你很快会得到 10 到 100 倍惊喜的原因，因为尽管 Jensen 希望优化，但下一个巨大的优化即将到来。你不需要乘法器，不需要 32 位数据。

turns.en: Definitely not the 32-bit. Well, that's a rare case where you use that.
turns.zh: 肯定不是 32 位。那是很少用的情况。

turns.en: Yeah. Rare. I think there's a I mean it does come out like sort of it's kind of like an address like state, city, and street. So if you're in context and you know if you know you're in Austin, you only need to specify the street. If you know that you know you know like if you know you're in this is where you get the information advantage like four bits is not normally enough but it would is enough if you already know where you are. Like if you already know you're in Austin, you only need four bits for the street.
turns.zh: 是的，很少。我想这有点像地址，比如州、城市和街道。所以如果你在上下文中，知道你在奥斯汀，你只需要指定街道。如果你知道你在哪里，这就是信息优势所在。通常 4 位不够，但如果你已经知道你在哪里，那就够了。比如你已经知道你在奥斯汀，你只需要 4 位来表示街道。

turns.en: Yeah. you know if you know you're in Texas then you need to say okay which city it's state city street this year that's how you get to the four bit thing they're going to right now dependent we use the we train on 16 bit and we compress down to four at inference time
turns.zh: 是的，如果你知道你在德克萨斯州，那么你需要说哪个城市，就是州、城市、街道，这就是你得到 4 位的方法。目前我们依赖，我们用 16 位训练，推理时压缩到 4 位。

turns.en: No doubt in my mind this year we're going to flip to training on four or even less and it's going to a massive step up in perform. I think the way it'll end up is the GB300s will be here and there'll be a co-processor that has, you know, maybe 2,000 or 4,000 cores that are tiny. They don't handle anything other than 4bit on down. And that combination is going to give us a 10 to 100x and that's going to push every and then then it'll be self-designing its own chips after that. And it just skyrockets from there.
turns.zh: 毫无疑问，今年我们将转向用 4 位甚至更低位训练，这将带来巨大的性能提升。我认为最终的结果是 GB300 会到来，并且会有一个协处理器，可能有 2000 或 4000 个小核心，只处理 4 位及以下的数据。这种组合将给我们带来 10 到 100 倍的提升，然后推动一切，之后它会自己设计芯片。然后就从那里起飞。

turns.en: Infinite self improvement. Well, like the robots building themselves, but much sooner because it's all just go to TSMC, make this instead, come back. 90-day lag.
turns.zh: 无限自我改进。就像机器人自己建造自己，但更快，因为只需要去台积电，制造这个，回来。90 天的延迟。

turns.en: I think the next year alone is going to be almost unfathomable. I think next year is going to feel like the future.
turns.zh: 我认为仅明年一年就几乎难以想象。我觉得明年会感觉像未来。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: More than any other year. I mean, the past year or two has been a lot of interesting digital elements, but when we've got, you know, humanoid robots moving around and we have the cyber cab driving around and we have, you know, flying cars, drones, it's going to feel like the future. We're going to have the jetins sort of materializing before us by the end of next year, I think. So, yeah.
turns.zh: 比任何一年都更像未来。过去一两年有很多有趣的数字元素，但当我们有了人形机器人四处走动，有了赛博出租车到处跑，还有飞行汽车、无人机，那就会感觉像未来了。我想到明年年底，喷气背包之类的东西也会出现在我们面前。

turns.en: And we have rockets flying in big time.
turns.zh: 还有火箭大规模飞行。

turns.en: Yeah. Like the robot production will scale very it'll be there'll be a shitload of robots basically in two years.
turns.zh: 是的。机器人生产将大规模扩展，基本上两年内会有大量机器人。

turns.en: It's a defined unit of measure.
turns.zh: 这是一个明确的计量单位。

turns.en: It won't be rare.
turns.zh: 不会稀少了。

turns.en: Yeah. Well, will you offer any optimize for home purchase? Will you sell or only lease the robots, do you think?
turns.zh: 是的。你会为家庭购买提供任何优化吗？你认为你会出售还是只出租机器人？

turns.en: I don't know yet. There will be initially a scarcity of robots and then there will be robots will be plentiful. So the time gap between scarce and plentiful will be only a matter of five years.
turns.zh: 我还不知道。最初机器人会稀缺，然后会变得充足。所以从稀缺到充足的时间差距只有五年。

turns.en: You know how the Tesla comes to your driveway now and you just buy it online and it just drives up to you. Will the robot just come to ring the doorbell too? probably it gets out of the Tesla and comes up. Right.
turns.zh: 你知道现在特斯拉会开到你的车道上，你在线购买，它就直接开到你面前。机器人也会来按门铃吗？可能它从特斯拉里出来然后走过来。对。

turns.en: I mean, what I find fascinating, Elon, is the amount of compute that you're building into things that walk out of the factory, the cars and the robots, the amount of distributed inference compute that's going to be in the world.
turns.zh: 我觉得很迷人的是，你正在把大量算力构建到走出工厂的东西里，比如汽车和机器人，世界上将会有大量的分布式推理算力。

turns.en: A lot a lot. A lot a lot. Yeah. And that's one way to scale the AI is distributed edge compute. So I want to ask a question I don't want to hit any hot points but in one early on I think you imagined OpenAI as a counterbalance for Google. Is XAI now the counterbalance for Google?
turns.zh: 很多很多。是的。这是扩展 AI 的一种方式，即分布式边缘计算。所以我想问一个问题，不想触及敏感点，但早期我认为你设想 OpenAI 作为谷歌的制衡力量。现在 XAI 是谷歌的制衡力量吗？

turns.en: Yeah, probably.
turns.zh: 是的，很可能。

turns.en: I guess Anthropic is doing some good work, especially in coding. OpenAI has certainly done impressive work. I'm still stuck on how you go from a nonprofit open source to a profit-maximizing closed source—missing some of the parts in the middle. But they have done impressive things.
turns.zh: 我猜 Anthropic 在做一些不错的工作，尤其是在编程方面。OpenAI 确实做了令人印象深刻的工作。我仍然困惑于如何从非营利开源转向利润最大化的闭源——中间缺失了一些环节。但他们确实做了了不起的事情。

turns.en: Does anybody else appear on the horizon, or is it these players in China? Can somebody come out? To the best of my knowledge, my best guess is that it will be xAI and Google that will be the primacy.
turns.zh: 还有其他人出现吗，还是只有中国的这些玩家？有人能脱颖而出吗？据我所知，我最好的猜测是 xAI 和 Google 将占据主导地位。

turns.en: You know, what is the best AI? And then at some point, it's going to be a competition with China.
turns.zh: 你知道，什么是最好的 AI？然后在某个时候，这将是与中国的竞争。

turns.en: China's just got a lot of power. Like the electricity. I think China will pass three times the US electricity output in 2026. And they will figure out the chips.
turns.zh: 中国有大量的电力。我认为到 2026 年，中国的发电量将是美国的三倍。而且他们会搞定芯片。

turns.en: They're going to start chip manufacturing. Right.
turns.zh: 他们将开始芯片制造。没错。

turns.en: Yeah. They'll figure out the chips. As it is, there are diminishing returns to the chips at this point. You go from 3 nanometer to 2 nanometer, you don't get a 3:2 ratio improvement. You get like a 10% improvement.
turns.zh: 是的。他们会搞定芯片。目前，芯片的收益递减。从 3 纳米到 2 纳米，你不会得到 3:2 的改进比例。你只能得到大约 10% 的改进。

turns.en: Yeah. It's just diminishing returns on the chip size. And Jensen has said that Moore's law is dead. It's not like you can just make things smaller and make it better.
turns.zh: 是的。芯片尺寸的收益递减。Jensen 说过摩尔定律已死。你不能只是把东西做得更小就让它更好。

turns.en: Yeah. There's a discrete number of atoms.
turns.zh: 是的。原子数量是离散的。

turns.en: That's why I think you should just stop talking nanometers and say how many atoms and what location, because this is marketing BS. So that makes it easier for China to catch up, because with every wall, everybody has limitation.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么我认为你应该停止谈论纳米，而说有多少原子以及它们的位置，因为这是营销废话。所以这让中国更容易追赶，因为每堵墙，每个人都有局限性。

turns.en: Yeah. It's like no one has plans to use the 5000 series ASML machines, right? Those cost twice as much and can only do half a reticle. They probably have some improvements in the works, but it's basically half the chip for twice the cost for a gain that is relatively small.
turns.zh: 是的。就像没有人计划使用 ASML 5000 系列机器，对吧？那些机器成本翻倍，却只能处理半个掩模版。他们可能有一些改进在进行中，但基本上是用两倍的成本得到一半的芯片，收益相对较小。

turns.en: So, anyway, the point is that China is going to have more power than anyone else and probably will have more chips.
turns.zh: 所以，无论如何，关键是中国的电力将超过任何其他国家，并且可能拥有更多的芯片。

turns.en: It's a great insight because I think a lot of people are used to the chip wars where I'm running single-threaded code. I need the CPU to double in speed and I can increase the price, but I need that out in an 18-month cycle time or less. We've been doing that for so long that nobody can see that it doesn't matter. You can buy Intel or you can build your own fabs and you can use them for a much longer period of time.
turns.zh: 这是一个很好的见解，因为我认为很多人习惯了芯片战争，我运行单线程代码。我需要 CPU 速度翻倍，我可以提高价格，但我需要在 18 个月或更短的周期内实现。我们这样做太久了，以至于没人能看到这并不重要。你可以买 Intel 或者自己建晶圆厂，并且可以使用更长时间。

turns.en: Oh yeah. Absolutely. Much longer. I totally agree. In fact, our AI4 chip is relatively primitive at this point. The same fab that makes that, if we apply the AI6 logic design to the fab—which is a nominally 5 nanometer fab—we can easily get an order of magnitude better output in the same fab.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。绝对如此。长得多。我完全同意。事实上，我们的 AI4 芯片目前相对原始。如果我们将 AI6 逻辑设计应用于同一晶圆厂——名义上是 5 纳米晶圆厂——我们很容易在同一晶圆厂获得一个数量级的更好输出。

turns.en: Yeah. And the other thing concurrent with that is the volume. If you 50x the number of chips, can you do something useful with it? You used to not be able to. You'd be like, well, now I've got five CPUs, but I still have the same single-threaded code. What am I going to do with five Excel spreadsheets side by side? Now it's like, no, I can translate that into useful intelligence instantaneously.
turns.zh: 是的。与此并行的是数量。如果你将芯片数量增加 50 倍，你能用它做有用的事情吗？你以前不能。你会想，好吧，现在我有五个 CPU，但我仍然有相同的单线程代码。我拿五个并排的 Excel 电子表格做什么？现在不是了，我可以瞬间将其转化为有用的智能。

turns.en: Exactly. It's not constrained by humans. It's not a human productivity amplifier. It's an independent productivity generator.
turns.zh: 完全正确。它不受人类限制。它不是人类生产力的放大器。它是一个独立的生产力生成器。

turns.en: Dead right. So many people have missed the importance of this. And this is where China, you know, China makes far more solar panels than we do. And we're like, well, actually, it's a crazy degree.
turns.zh: 说得太对了。很多人忽略了这一点的重要性。这就是中国的优势，你知道，中国生产的太阳能电池板比我们多得多。我们觉得，嗯，实际上，这程度很疯狂。

turns.en: Crazy degree. If they do that in chips, you're like, well, but who cares? They're 7 nanometer. Like, oh, no. It's wrong.
turns.zh: 疯狂的程度。如果他们用芯片也这样做，你会想，好吧，但谁在乎呢？它们是 7 纳米。就像，哦，不。这是错的。

turns.en: Yes. Correct. Based on current trends, China will far exceed the rest of the world in AI compute.
turns.zh: 是的。正确。根据当前趋势，中国在 AI 算力上将远远超过世界其他地区。

turns.en: So what happens then? You've got xAI and Google and China Inc. Let's call it that for the moment. And you've got massive amounts of ASI-level compute that frankly, the only thing that understands the other ASI-level compute is the ASI here. Can they all just play together? Is it Darwinian? There might be some Darwinian element to it.
turns.zh: 那么接下来会发生什么？你有 xAI、Google 和中国公司——我们暂时这么称呼。你有大量的 ASI 级别算力，坦率地说，唯一能理解其他 ASI 级别算力的就是这里的 ASI。它们能一起玩吗？是达尔文式的吗？可能有一些达尔文式的元素。

turns.en: Let's look on the bright side of life. I bring Grok out to speak to us again.
turns.zh: 让我们看看生活光明的一面。我让 Grok 再次对我们说话。

turns.en: I don't know. There's just going to be a lot of intelligence. Like a lot. Now the ratio of human intelligence asymptotically falls to 0% on the planet.
turns.zh: 我不知道。将会有大量的智能。非常多。现在人类智能的比例在地球上渐近地降到 0%。

turns.en: Yeah, pretty much.
turns.zh: 是的，差不多。

turns.en: Pretty much. Several years ago I said humans are the biological bootloader for digital superintelligence.
turns.zh: 差不多。几年前我说过，人类是数字超级智能的生物引导程序。

turns.en: Yes, we are a transitional species.
turns.zh: 是的，我们是一个过渡物种。

turns.en: We're a bootloader. Yeah.
turns.zh: 我们是引导程序。是的。

turns.en: We are a transition.
turns.zh: 我们是一个过渡。

turns.en: I mean, silicon circuits can't evolve in a salt pond, you know.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，硅电路不能在盐池中进化，你知道。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: So you need a bootloader. We're the bootloader.
turns.zh: 所以你需要一个引导程序。我们就是引导程序。

turns.en: But you would never impair your bootloader.
turns.zh: 但你永远不会损害你的引导程序。

turns.en: Yeah. So, hope we've been a good bootloader.
turns.zh: 是的。所以，希望我们一直是个好的引导程序。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And it's nice to us in the future.
turns.zh: 而且未来对我们很好。

turns.en: Is this where we want to end the pod?
turns.zh: 我们想在这里结束播客吗？

turns.en: Most people don't know what a bootloader even is. Oh my god.
turns.zh: 大多数人甚至不知道引导程序是什么。哦，天哪。

turns.en: Yes. Yeah, boot discs are a far and distant memory.
turns.zh: 是的。启动盘是遥远的记忆了。

turns.en: Well, we can make an 'Always Look on the Bright Side of Life' clone song. Yeah, we can clone that and make that the closing theme. That'd be awesome.
turns.zh: 好吧，我们可以做一个《Always Look on the Bright Side of Life》的克隆歌曲。是的，我们可以克隆它，让它成为结尾曲。那太棒了。

turns.en: I'll go back to this is the most exciting time ever to be alive. The only time more exciting than today is tomorrow. And it's interesting that we're heading towards a world in which any single person can have their grandest dreams become true.
turns.zh: 我要回到这一点：这是有史以来最激动人心的活着的时候。唯一比今天更激动人心的时刻是明天。而且有趣的是，我们正走向一个任何个人都能实现最伟大梦想的世界。

turns.en: That's like Walt Disney word for word. You got to make that into a new exhibit.
turns.zh: 这简直就是沃尔特·迪斯尼的原话。你得把它做成一个新展览。

turns.en: Like I said, you asked about sci-fi that is a non-dystopian future, right? The Banks books are probably the best.
turns.zh: 就像我说的，你问过关于非反乌托邦未来的科幻小说，对吧？班克斯的书可能是最好的。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: You should pay a producer to go and make those.
turns.zh: 你应该花钱请制片人去制作那些。

turns.en: Those are the Culture books, which is Consider Phlebas, which is GG for my wife.
turns.zh: 那些是《文明》系列，包括《向风守望》，这是我妻子最喜欢的。

ts.sec: Discussing Iain M. Banks' Culture series

turns.en: I wonder, because she's like, 'What the hell are you reading?'
turns.zh: 我好奇，因为她会说：‘你他妈的读什么呢？’

turns.en: Well, the way Consider Phlebas starts out is a little... I mean, the whole thing is, he starts off being drowned. That's a good opening scene. We really...
turns.zh: 嗯，《向风守望》的开头有点……我是说，整本书就是，他一开始就被淹死了。那是个不错的开场。我们真的……

turns.en: Yeah. How do you not make that movie?
turns.zh: 是啊。你怎么不把那拍成电影？

turns.en: It can be a little off-putting to some people. Yeah. You need to get through the first few hundred pages.
turns.zh: 对有些人来说可能有点劝退。是啊。你得熬过前几百页。

turns.en: People don't walk out of a movie in the first five minutes though. They'll give it a chance. Like Player of Games might be a better book to start off with than Consider Phlebas.
turns.zh: 但人们不会在电影开头五分钟就离场。他们会给个机会。比如《游戏玩家》可能比《向风守望》更适合入门。

turns.en: That was one I enjoyed. Humans still exist in this future, which is a good thing.
turns.zh: 那本我很喜欢。在那个未来里人类还存在，这是好事。

turns.en: Yes, they do. A lot of humans. In that future there are trillions of humans.
turns.zh: 是的，存在。很多人。在那个未来里有数万亿人类。

turns.en: Well, we need to get the reproduction rate up.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们得提高生育率。

turns.en: By the way, my friend Ben Lamb's company, Colossal, is making artificial wombs. He's the company bringing back the woolly mammoth and the saber-tooth tiger and all of these.
turns.zh: 顺便说一句，我朋友 Ben Lamb 的公司 Colossal 正在制造人造子宫。他就是那家让猛犸象和剑齿虎复活的公司。

turns.en: When do we get... can we have a miniature pet woolly mammoth as a pet?
turns.zh: 什么时候我们能……能养一只迷你猛犸象当宠物吗？

turns.en: Okay. Well, he made the woolly mouse with tusks.
turns.zh: 好吧。他做了带象牙的毛茸茸老鼠。

turns.en: Wouldn't that be adorable?
turns.zh: 那不可爱吗？

turns.en: Yeah. It's just like licking you in the face.
turns.zh: 是啊。就像舔你的脸。

turns.en: Yeah. It's just sort of trotting around the house. What would your optimal size be? Be adorable.
turns.zh: 是啊。就在屋子里跑来跑去。你觉得多大最合适？会很可爱。

turns.en: You know what they've learned how to do is to... little tusks and everything.
turns.zh: 你知道他们学会了什么吗……小象牙什么的。

turns.en: A miniature woolly mammoth would be an epic pet. I mean, look what we did with wolves.
turns.zh: 迷你猛犸象会是史诗级的宠物。看看我们对狼做了什么。

turns.en: Yeah. He turned a wolf into a little dog. He brought back the direwolf as well.
turns.zh: 是啊。他把狼变成了小狗。他还复活了冰原狼。

turns.en: But he made the woolly mouse. There's a woolly mouse now with tusks?
turns.zh: 但他做了毛茸茸老鼠。现在有带象牙的毛茸茸老鼠？

turns.en: No tusks. Different gene or what?
turns.zh: 没有象牙。不同的基因还是什么？

turns.en: I was there. He's in Dallas. Not far. I was visiting him and he said, 'Our scientists are going to a tusk conference next week.'
turns.zh: 我当时在场。他在达拉斯。不远。我去看他，他说：‘我们的科学家下周要去参加一个象牙会议。’

turns.en: To talk about all the genes involved in tusk creation.
turns.zh: 讨论所有涉及象牙生成的基因。

turns.en: They want to put them on the mouse.
turns.zh: 他们想把那些基因放到老鼠身上。

turns.en: No, I don't want you to probably add it to the mouse. That'd be creepy until it looks like a mouse-sized woolly mammoth.
turns.zh: 不，我可能不想你把它加到老鼠身上。那会让人毛骨悚然，直到它看起来像老鼠大小的猛犸象。

turns.en: That's just going to freak people out. The little woolly mammoth will sell.
turns.zh: 那只会吓到人。小猛犸象会卖得好。

turns.en: Yeah. Tusk mouse will not sell.
turns.zh: 是啊。象牙老鼠卖不出去。

turns.en: Yeah. It's going to crush. I mean, too creepy.
turns.zh: 是啊。它会大卖。我是说，太诡异了。

turns.en: You thought Labradoodle was cool when you see the woolly mammoth.
turns.zh: 等你看到猛犸象，你会觉得拉布拉多犬都不算什么。

turns.en: Saber-tooth tiger would be good, too. Like a cat. Cat size.
turns.zh: 剑齿虎也不错。像猫一样。猫的大小。

turns.en: Those teeth come down to like here. I don't know how they actually bite, but they did. Did they actually bite with those things? I don't think I opened them.
turns.zh: 那些牙齿垂到这儿。我不知道它们怎么咬，但它们确实咬了。它们真的用那些东西咬吗？我不觉得我张开了嘴。

turns.en: Not my... the teeth seem kind of unwieldy, you know?
turns.zh: 不是我的……那些牙齿看起来有点笨重，你知道吗？

turns.en: Yeah, they're just for show. They look good. They're like jewelry.
turns.zh: 是啊，它们只是装饰。好看。就像珠宝。

turns.en: But no dinosaurs.
turns.zh: 但没有恐龙。

turns.en: No dinosaur or not?
turns.zh: 没有恐龙还是不？

turns.en: I think Jurassic Park's a great idea. I mean, really, you didn't see the end of the movie. Eyes will help us with that.
turns.zh: 我觉得《侏罗纪公园》是个好主意。我是说，真的，你没看到电影的结局。眼睛会帮我们解决这个问题。

turns.en: Nothing's perfect. Oh, yeah. That really will.
turns.zh: 没有什么是完美的。哦，是啊。那确实会。

turns.en: I mean, if there was an island with a whole bunch of dinosaurs, 100%.
turns.zh: 我是说，如果有个岛全是恐龙，百分之百。

turns.en: Yes. I'd pay a lot for that.
turns.zh: 是的。我会付很多钱。

turns.en: And it's like once in a while somebody gets chomped by a dinosaur. You're like, 'What's...' you know, it's one in a million. I'll still go.
turns.zh: 而且偶尔有人被恐龙咬死。你会想，‘什么……’你知道，那是百万分之一的事。我还是会去。

turns.en: Who are they missing? Lysine.
turns.zh: 他们缺什么？赖氨酸。

turns.en: No. The oldest DNA that's been recovered is like 1.2 million years.
turns.zh: 不。恢复的最古老 DNA 大约有 120 万年。

turns.en: Oh, you can just wing it though. Just make it look like that. Whatever.
turns.zh: 哦，但你可以随便弄。就让它看起来像那样。随便。

turns.en: This would be one of the... Actually, that was my proposed X Prize. Remember back in visioneering?
turns.zh: 这会是……实际上，那是我提议的 X Prize。还记得在 visioneering 的时候吗？

turns.en: What's that?
turns.zh: 那是什么？

turns.en: Take the DNA strand and predict what it'll look like.
turns.zh: 拿 DNA 链预测它会是什么样子。

turns.en: Yeah. Exactly. And then just reverse engineer the dinosaurs.
turns.zh: 是啊。完全正确。然后反向工程恐龙。

turns.en: It would be funny if there were two completely different DNA strands. They're like, 'Well, they both look like T-Rex.'
turns.zh: 如果有两条完全不同的 DNA 链，那会很有趣。他们会说：‘嗯，它们看起来都像霸王龙。’

turns.en: Is T-Rex real or is that like an assembly? I mean, it's nice to believe it's real, but the front legs were from a completely different dinosaur.
turns.zh: 霸王龙是真的还是拼凑的？我是说，相信它是真的很好，但前腿来自完全不同的恐龙。

turns.en: That was the one at eight. It actually had huge front legs.
turns.zh: 那是八点钟的那个。它实际上有巨大的前腿。

turns.en: There's something wrong with the arms.
turns.zh: 手臂有问题。

turns.en: I don't buy it on the arms front. The many arms seem implausible.
turns.zh: 我不相信手臂方面。多只手臂似乎不可信。

turns.en: Nope. Well, DNA will tell us. We'll know in a year.
turns.zh: 不。好吧，DNA 会告诉我们。一年后我们就知道了。

turns.en: The future is going to be Jurassic Island.
turns.zh: 未来会是侏罗纪岛。

turns.en: We say, 'Wow.'
turns.zh: 我们会说：‘哇。’

turns.en: No, no, I meant the amino acid that the dinosaurs were missing that kept them from reproducing.
turns.zh: 不，不，我指的是恐龙缺少的那种氨基酸，阻止了它们繁殖。

turns.en: What? Lysine, you're saying?
turns.zh: 什么？你是说赖氨酸？

turns.en: Was it lysine? I forget what it was. But no, the dinosaurs got held back by something like an asteroid bombardment.
turns.zh: 是赖氨酸吗？我忘了是什么。但不，恐龙是被像小行星撞击之类的东西阻止的。

turns.en: Right. They were doing great. 60 million years. We got very lucky. They had a great much longer.
turns.zh: 对。它们过得很好。6000 万年。我们非常幸运。它们过得很好，更久。

turns.en: See, there's a good argument why there's no other intelligence out there. There's plenty of dinosaurs in the universe.
turns.zh: 看，这是一个很好的论点，为什么没有其他智慧存在。宇宙中有很多恐龙。

turns.en: What were we back then? Like a vole or something?
turns.zh: 我们那时是什么？像田鼠之类的？

turns.en: We were very good at hiding.
turns.zh: 我们非常擅长躲藏。

turns.en: It is amazing. We went from a little rat, little mole to us in 60 million years. Doesn't seem that long. That's why no one believed Darwin.
turns.zh: 这太神奇了。我们从一只小老鼠、小鼹鼠变成我们，用了 6000 万年。看起来没那么长。这就是为什么没人相信达尔文。

turns.en: It's like doesn't seem plausible. It's a long time. 60 million. It turns out it is.
turns.zh: 看起来不太可信。那是一段很长的时间。6000 万年。结果证明是的。

turns.en: You're making robots, but it's interesting. I think it'll be a lot more interesting to design biological robots, like a little cat that goes around and pees stain remover and eats lint off the carpet. That's going to be an interesting...
turns.zh: 你在造机器人，但这很有趣。我觉得设计生物机器人会更有趣，比如一只小猫，到处撒尿去污剂，吃地毯上的绒毛。那会很有趣……

turns.en: But you have a mechanical like an Optimus doing that anyway.
turns.zh: 但你有机械的，比如 Optimus，反正也能做那些。

turns.en: Yeah. Well, they went bankrupt, so we'll have to build this.
turns.zh: 是啊。好吧，他们破产了，所以我们得造这个。

turns.en: I think you can still buy them, though.
turns.zh: 不过我觉得你还是能买到它们。

turns.en: Anyway, the room is basically that... it's going to be... but the thing is, a human robot is general purpose, so it can do whatever you want.
turns.zh: 不管怎样，房间基本上就是……它会……但关键是，人形机器人是通用的，所以它能做任何你想做的事。

turns.en: Yeah, they were too early. No vision system, no GB300.
turns.zh: 是啊，它们太早了。没有视觉系统，没有 GB300。

turns.en: How do you build a Roomba that works?
turns.zh: 你怎么造一个能用的 Roomba？

turns.en: I think the idea of having an Optimus vacuum is like the most underused asset. It could, but it can just do anything.
turns.zh: 我觉得把 Optimus 当吸尘器用是最被低估的用途。它能做到，而且它什么都能干。

turns.en: It can. Yes, of course.
turns.zh: 它确实可以。当然。

turns.en: And you can mass manufacture at, you know, one.
turns.zh: 而且你可以大规模制造，你知道，一台。

turns.en: Oh, that's yeah. Optimus, build me a Roomba. That's what you'll do. You want to say, Optimus, vacuum, carve it, Optimus, build me a Roomba that vacuums.
turns.zh: 哦，没错。Optimus，给我造个 Roomba。这就是你要做的。你想说，Optimus，吸尘，雕刻它，Optimus，给我造个能吸尘的 Roomba。

turns.en: Build a house. Build me a robot.
turns.zh: 造房子。给我造个机器人。

turns.en: It's going to be a lot of robots.
turns.zh: 那会有很多机器人。

turns.en: Maybe we should do this once a year. Checkpoint.
turns.zh: 也许我们应该每年做一次。检查点。

turns.en: I would like that. Checkpoint. That's going to be we can roll back the...
turns.zh: 我喜欢这个主意。检查点。这样我们就可以回滚……

turns.en: What were we saying predictions last year?
turns.zh: 我们去年说了什么预测？

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。对。

turns.en: All right. Well, we can always control it. We can cut out the bus.
turns.zh: 好吧。我们总能控制它。我们可以剪掉那段。

turns.en: Are you selling hope?
turns.zh: 你在卖希望吗？

turns.en: As a matter of fact, it worked out really well.
turns.zh: 事实上，效果很好。

turns.en: You pull up in your Tesla like, 'Hey, I bought this with my dollars per hope.' You know,
turns.zh: 你开着特斯拉过来，说：‘嘿，我用我的每美元希望买了这个。’你知道，

turns.en: I'll send you the mug.
turns.zh: 我会给你寄杯子。

turns.en: Monetize hope.
turns.zh: 把希望变现。

turns.en: All right. Monetize Hope. One year from today, December 22nd, I'll come and knock on the door right here. If you're here, you're here. If you're not, we'll talk about you.
turns.zh: 好吧。把希望变现。一年后的今天，12 月 22 日，我会来敲这里的门。如果你在，你就在。如果你不在，我们就聊你。

turns.en: I mean, a year from now, we might have the new Optimus factory where the building will be built.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，一年后，我们可能会有新的 Optimus 工厂，建筑会建起来。

turns.en: That would be awesome. 8 million square feet of robots running.
turns.zh: 那太棒了。800 万平方英尺的机器人在运行。

turns.en: It's going to be a giant giant building.
turns.zh: 那会是一个巨大的建筑。

turns.en: Oh, man. Yeah, they freak me out when they're recharging. It's like hang in there. It's like what's wrong with that thing?
turns.zh: 哦，天哪。是啊，它们充电的时候吓到我了。就像‘坚持住’，‘那东西怎么了？’

turns.en: Yeah, we're actually just going to have them like I think sit down.
turns.zh: 是啊，我们实际上会让它们坐下来。

turns.en: As opposed to look like some sort of...
turns.zh: 而不是看起来像某种……

turns.en: They need like a recharging cigar.
turns.zh: 它们需要一个充电雪茄。

turns.en: A recharging cigar. Less morg like...
turns.zh: 充电雪茄。不那么像停尸房……

turns.en: Snapping here with a book.
turns.zh: 拿着书在这里啪嗒啪嗒。

turns.en: Yeah, that'd be much better. Right now they're just like literally like is it dead? Just limp.
turns.zh: 是啊，那会好多了。现在它们简直就像‘它死了吗？’只是软塌塌的。

turns.en: Yeah, that's a good point. That's a big contribution from this particular brand.
turns.zh: 是啊，说得好。这是这个特定品牌的一大贡献。

turns.en: All right. Till next year then.
turns.zh: 好吧。那明年见。

turns.en: All right. It's a day.
turns.zh: 好的。一言为定。

turns.en: Thanks, buddy.
turns.zh: 谢谢，伙计。

turns.en: Awesome, guys.
turns.zh: 太棒了，各位。

turns.en: If you made it to the end of this episode, which you obviously did, I consider you a moonshot mate. Every week, my moonshot mates and I spend a lot of energy and time to really deliver you the news that matters. If you're a subscriber, thank you. If you're not a subscriber yet, please consider subscribing so you get the news as it comes out. I also want to invite you to join me on my weekly newsletter called Metatrends. I have a research team. You may not know this, but we spend the entire week looking at the meta trends that are impacting your family, your company, your industry, your nation. And I put this into a two-minute read every week. If you'd like to get access to the MetaTrens newsletter every week, go to diamandis.com/metatrends. That's diamandis.com/metatrens. Thank you again for joining us today. It's a blast for us to put this together every week.
turns.zh: 如果你听到了这期节目的结尾——显然你做到了——我把你视为登月伙伴。每周，我和我的登月伙伴们花费大量精力和时间，为你带来真正重要的新闻。如果你已经是订阅者，谢谢。如果你还不是订阅者，请考虑订阅，这样你就能第一时间收到新闻。我还想邀请你加入我的每周通讯《Metatrends》。我有一个研究团队，你可能不知道，我们花整周时间研究那些影响你家庭、公司、行业和国家的宏观趋势。我每周把它写成一篇两分钟能读完的文章。如果你想每周获取《Metatrends》通讯，请访问 diamandis.com/metatrends。那是 diamandis.com/metatrends。再次感谢你今天加入我们。每周制作这期节目对我们来说非常愉快。

consensus.en: We are in the singularity now, with AI and robotics accelerating exponentially.
consensus.zh: 我们现在正处于奇点之中，AI 和机器人技术正在指数级加速。

consensus.en: Universal high income will come from AI-driven deflation, not redistribution.
consensus.zh: 全民高收入将来自 AI 驱动的通缩，而非再分配。

consensus.en: Energy is the innermost loop; solar and batteries are key to abundance.
consensus.zh: 能源是核心内循环；太阳能和电池是实现富足的关键。

consensus.en: AI will surpass all human intelligence combined by 2030.
consensus.zh: 到 2030 年，AI 将超越所有人类智能的总和。

consensus.en: Truth, curiosity, and beauty are essential values to program into AI for a benign future.
consensus.zh: 真理、好奇心和美感是编程进 AI 以实现良性未来的核心价值。

contrarian.en: China will far exceed the rest of the world in AI compute, running circles around the US.
contrarian.zh: 中国在 AI 算力上将远超世界其他地区，把美国远远甩在后面。

contrarian.en: Fusion on Earth is farcical; the sun is the only fusion reactor we need.
contrarian.zh: 在地球上搞核聚变是荒谬的；太阳才是我们唯一需要的聚变反应堆。

contrarian.en: College is mostly for social experience; curriculum can't keep up with technology.
contrarian.zh: 上大学主要是为了社交体验；课程内容跟不上技术发展。

contrarian.en: Longevity escape velocity is near; aging is a solvable software problem.
contrarian.zh: 长寿逃逸速度即将到来；衰老是一个可解决的软件问题。

contrarian.en: The speed of light prevents a single ASI from dominating; multiple AIs will coexist.
contrarian.zh: 光速限制阻止单一超级智能统治；多个 AI 将共存。

contrarian.en: Saving for retirement will be irrelevant in 10-20 years due to AI-driven abundance.
contrarian.zh: 由于 AI 驱动的富足，10-20 年内为退休储蓄将毫无意义。
